# Skill: gitlab-coding-principles

# Load Project Principles

Evaluate ALL groups below and load principles from EVERY group that applies to
your task. Most tasks span multiple groups (e.g., a model change may need
Backend, Database, and Testing principles). DO NOT stop after the first group.
When your task involves database queries, scopes, or data access patterns,
ALWAYS load Database principles regardless of which files you are editing.

**Database:**
- **Database fundamentals (model queries, batching, sharding, partitioning, N+1)**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/database-fundamentals.md *(load for any database work)*
- **Database migration patterns and zero-downtime safety**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/database-migrations.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/database-fundamentals.md)*
- **Column types, constraints, indexes, naming conventions**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/database-schema.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/database-fundamentals.md)*
- **SQL performance, transactions, batching**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/database-queries.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/database-fundamentals.md)*
- **ClickHouse database concerns**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/clickhouse.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/database-fundamentals.md)*

**Cells:**
- **Cells architecture sharding keys, organization data ownership, routable requests, and cell isolation when adding tables or customer-owned resources**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/cells-fundamentals.md *(load for any cells work)*
- **Cells globally-unique attribute claims (Cells::Claimable), claim rollout lifecycle, and feature flags for cross-cell uniqueness**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/cells-claims.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/cells-fundamentals.md)*

**Security:**
- **Security vulnerabilities and secure coding**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/security.md
- **Authentication, authorization, token handling, OAuth, SAML, identity linking, composite identity, session management, 2FA, MFA, password management**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/authentication.md

**Permissions:**
- **Where to check permissions, naming conventions, role definitions**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/permissions-fundamentals.md *(load for any permissions work)*
- **Granular PAT (GPAT) compliance for GraphQL types and mutations**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/permissions-graphql-gpat.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/permissions-fundamentals.md)*
- **Granular PAT (GPAT) compliance for REST endpoints and job tokens**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/permissions-rest-gpat.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/permissions-fundamentals.md)*

**Code Review:**
- **General code review practices and acceptance checklist**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/code-review.md

**Backend:**
- **Ruby/Rails style, logging, common pitfalls**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/backend-ruby.md *(load for any backend work)*
- **Service patterns, abstractions, design conventions**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/backend-architecture.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/backend-ruby.md)*
- **EE/CE separation, licensing, code placement**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/backend-ee.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/backend-ruby.md)*

**API:**
- **REST API design and conventions**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/rest-api.md
- **GraphQL API design and conventions**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/graphql.md

**Workers:**
- **Sidekiq worker design and reliability**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/workers.md

**CI/CD:**
- **GitLab CI/CD internals — adding pipeline configuration keywords (Ci::Config::Entry classes, feature-flag gating, JSON schema) and creating CI routing/partitioned tables. NOTE this is about GitLab's own CI implementation, not authoring a project's .gitlab-ci.yml**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/cicd-internals.md

**Frontend:**
- **Vue.js components, state management, patterns**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-vue.md *(load for any frontend work)*
- **CSS/SCSS, Tailwind, dark mode**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-style.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-vue.md)*
- **HAML templates, ViewComponents, Pajamas**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-haml.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-vue.md)*
- **Frontend accessibility patterns and requirements**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-a11y.md *(also load: .ai/principles/distilled/frontend-vue.md)*

**Testing:**
- **RSpec patterns, factories, shared examples**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/qa-rspec.md
- **Jest, jsdom, Vue Test Utils patterns**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/qa-jest.md

**Performance:**
- **Performance and scalability**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/performance.md

**Documentation:**
- **Documentation style and completeness**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/documentation.md

**Feature Flags:**
- **Feature flag usage and lifecycle**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/feature-flags.md

**Analytics:**
- **Analytics instrumentation and metrics**: Read .ai/principles/distilled/analytics.md

- **Code style or linting**: Read .ai/code-style.md
- **Git, commits, or branches**: Read .ai/git.md
- **CI/CD pipelines or `.gitlab-ci.yml`**: Read .ai/ci-cd.md

1: # GitLab
2: 
3: ## Canonical source
4: 
5: The canonical source of GitLab where all development takes place is [hosted on GitLab.com](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab).
6: 
7: If you wish to clone a copy of GitLab without proprietary code, you can use the read-only mirror of GitLab located at https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-foss/. However, please do not submit any issues and/or merge requests to that project.
8: 
9: ## Free trial
10: 
11: You can request a free trial of GitLab Ultimate [on our website](https://about.gitlab.com/free-trial/).
12: 
13: ## Open source software to collaborate on code
14: 
15: To see how GitLab looks please see the [features page on our website](https://about.gitlab.com/features/).
16: 
17: - Manage Git repositories with fine grained access controls that keep your code secure
18: - Perform code reviews and enhance collaboration with merge requests
19: - Complete continuous integration (CI) and continuous deployment/delivery (CD) pipelines to build, test, and deploy your applications
20: - Each project can also have an issue tracker, issue board, and a wiki
21: - Used by more than 100,000 organizations, GitLab is the most popular solution to manage Git repositories on-premises
22: - Open-core, most of the code is MIT with the files in `/ee` being proprietary but source available and open for contributions.
23: 
24: ## Editions
25: 
26: There are three editions of GitLab:
27: 
28: - GitLab Community Edition (CE) is available freely under the MIT Expat license.
29: - GitLab Enterprise Edition (EE) includes [extra features](https://about.gitlab.com/pricing/#compare-options) that are more useful for organizations with more than 100 users. To use EE and get official support please [become a subscriber](https://about.gitlab.com/pricing/).
30: - JiHu Edition (JH) tailored specifically for the [Chinese market](https://about.gitlab.cn/).
31: 
32: ## Licensing
33: 
34: See the [LICENSE](LICENSE) file for licensing information as it pertains to
35: files in this repository.
36: 
37: ## Hiring
38: 
39: We are hiring developers, support engineers, and production engineers all the time, please see our [jobs page](https://about.gitlab.com/jobs/).
40: 
41: ## Website
42: 
43: On [about.gitlab.com](https://about.gitlab.com/) you can find more information about:
44: 
45: - [Subscriptions](https://about.gitlab.com/pricing/)
46: - [Professional Services](https://about.gitlab.com/services/)
47: - [Community](https://about.gitlab.com/community/)
48: - [Hosted GitLab.com](https://about.gitlab.com/gitlab-com/) use GitLab as a free service
49: - [GitLab Enterprise Edition](https://about.gitlab.com/features/#enterprise) with additional features aimed at larger organizations.
50: - [GitLab CI](https://about.gitlab.com/solutions/continuous-integration/) a continuous integration (CI) server that is easy to integrate with GitLab.
51: 
52: ## Requirements
53: 
54: Please see the [requirements documentation](doc/install/requirements.md) for system requirements and more information about the supported operating systems.
55: 
56: ## Installation
57: 
58: The recommended way to install GitLab is with the [Omnibus packages](https://docs.gitlab.com/install/package/) on our package server.
59: Compared to [a self-compiled installation](https://docs.gitlab.com/install/self_compiled/), this is faster and less error prone.
60: Just select your operating system, download the respective package (Debian or RPM) and install it using the system's package manager.
61: 
62: For more information about other installation methods, see [install GitLab](https://about.gitlab.com/install/).
63: 
64: ## Contributing
65: 
66: GitLab is an open source project and we are very happy to accept community contributions. Please refer to the [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](CONTRIBUTING.md) file for more details.
67: 
68: ## Install a development environment
69: 
70: To work on GitLab itself, we recommend setting up your development environment with [the GitLab Development Kit (GDK)](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-development-kit).
71: If you do not use GDK, you need to [install and configure all the dependencies yourself](https://docs.gitlab.com/install/installation/), this is a lot of work and error prone.
72: One small thing you also have to do when installing it yourself is to copy the example development Puma configuration file:
73: 
74: ```shell
75: cp config/puma.example.development.rb config/puma.rb
76: ```
77: 
78: Instructions on how to start GitLab and how to run the tests can be found in the [getting started section of the GitLab Development Kit](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-development-kit#getting-started).
79: 
80: ## Software stack
81: 
82: GitLab is a Ruby on Rails application that runs on the following software:
83: 
84: - Ubuntu/Debian/CentOS/RHEL/OpenSUSE
85: - Ruby (MRI) 3.3.10
86: - Git 2.33+
87: - Redis 6.0+
88: - PostgreSQL 16.5+
89: 
90: For more information please see the [architecture](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/development/architecture.html) and [requirements](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/install/requirements.html) documentation.
91: 
92: ## UX design
93: 
94: Please adhere to the [UX Guide](https://design.gitlab.com/) when creating designs and implementing code.
95: 
96: ## Third-party applications
97: 
98: There are a lot of [third-party applications integrating with GitLab](https://about.gitlab.com/applications/). These include GUI Git clients, mobile applications and API wrappers for various languages.
99: 
100: ## GitLab release cycle
101: 
102: For more information about the release process see the [release documentation](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/release-tools/blob/master/README.md).
103: 
104: ## Upgrading
105: 
106: For upgrading information please see our [update page](https://about.gitlab.com/update/).
107: 
108: ## Documentation
109: 
110: All documentation can be found on <https://docs.gitlab.com>.
111: 
112: ## Education
113: 
114: Get up to speed quickly with comprehensive learning resources at [GitLab University](https://university.gitlab.com).
115: 
116: ## Getting help
117: 
118: Please see [Getting help for GitLab](https://about.gitlab.com/getting-help/) on our website for the many options to get help.
119: 
120: ## Why should I use GitLab?
121: 
122: Read [why our customers choose GitLab](https://about.gitlab.com/why-gitlab/).

### Step 1: Orient in the codebase

This project is GitLab, a Ruby on Rails application for managing Git repositories with features like merge requests, CI/CD pipelines, and issue tracking. The codebase is organized into:
- `app/models/` for domain models
- `app/services/` for business logic
- `app/workers/` for background jobs
- `app/graphql/` for GraphQL API
- `lib/` for shared utilities

The central model representing a proposed code change is `MergeRequest` at `app/models/merge_request.rb:3`. It inherits from `ApplicationRecord` and includes concerns like `Issuable`, `Noteable`, and `Referable`.

### Step 2: Map the MergeRequest contract and what it is built on

The `MergeRequest` model at `app/models/merge_request.rb:3` exposes:
- Associations: `target_project`, `source_project`, `merge_user`, `merge_request_diffs`, `notes`, `metrics`, `merge_data`
- Callbacks: `before_validation`, `after_create`, `after_update`, `after_save`, `after_commit`
- Validations: branch validation, fork validation, target project validation
- Scopes: `by_source_or_target_branch`, `merged`, `non_closed`, `drafts`
- Methods: `merge_request_diff`, `diff_head_sha`, `mergeable?`, `merge!`, `rebase!`

Shared behavior pulled in through concerns:
- `Issuable` (`app/models/concerns/issuable.rb:9`): Common issuable behavior
- `Noteable` (`app/channels/noteable/notes_channel.rb:3`): Note/discussion functionality
- `Referable` (`app/models/concerns/referable.rb:7`): Reference extraction
- `Approvable` (`app/models/concerns/approvable.rb:3`): Approval workflows
- `Todoable` (`app/models/concerns/todoable.rb:9`): Todo tracking

Key dependencies:
- `MergeRequestDiff` (`app/models/merge_request_diff.rb:3`): Diff calculation
- `Note` (`app/models/note.rb:6`): Comments/discussions
- `Event` (`app/models/event.rb:3`): Activity tracking
- `Ci::Pipeline` (`app/models/ci/pipeline.rb:4`): CI pipeline integration

### Step 3: Trace how a merge request is created and updated

Creation flow:
1. Controller (`Projects::MergeRequestsController`) receives request
2. Service (`MergeRequests::CreateService`) validates and creates MR
3. Callbacks trigger:
   - `ensure_merge_request_diff` (`app/models/merge_request.rb:1456`) creates initial diff
   - `save_merge_data_changes` (`app/models/merge_request.rb:3105`) persists metadata
   - Event creation (`app/models/event.rb:3`) for activity feed

Update flow:
1. Controller (`Projects::MergeRequestsController`) receives update
2. Service (`MergeRequests::UpdateService`) applies changes
3. Callbacks trigger:
   - `clear_memoized_shas` (`app/models/merge_request.rb:1511`) invalidates caches
   - `reload_diff_if_branch_changed` (`app/models/merge_request.rb:1519`) refreshes diff
   - `enqueue_keep_around_commit` async worker preserves commits

Inline operations:
- Diff calculation (`app/models/merge_request_diff.rb:3`)
- Validation (`app/models/concerns/issuable.rb:9`)

Async operations:
- `MergeRequests::CleanupRefWorker` (`app/workers/merge_requests/cleanup_ref_worker.rb:26`)
- `MergeRequests::Refresh::NotifyAboutPushWorker` (`app/workers/merge_requests/refresh/notify_about_push_worker.rb:15`)

**Models:**
- `Event` (`app/models/event.rb:3`): Tracks MR activity
- `MergeRequestDiff` (`app/models/merge_request_diff.rb:3`): Diff storage
- `Note` (`app/models/note.rb:6`): Comments/discussions
- `Ci::Pipeline` (`app/models/ci/pipeline.rb:4`): CI integration
- `DeploymentMergeRequest` (`app/models/deployment_merge_request.rb:3`): Deployment tracking

**Services:**
- `MergeRequests::CreateService`: MR creation
- `MergeRequests::UpdateService`: MR updates
- `MergeRequests::MergeService` (`app/services/merge_requests/merge_service.rb`): Merge execution
- `Notes::CreateService`: Comment creation
- `Todos::CreateService`: Todo generation

**Workers:**
- `MergeRequests::CleanupRefWorker` (`app/workers/merge_requests/cleanup_ref_worker.rb:26`): Ref cleanup
- `MergeRequests::Refresh::NotifyAboutPushWorker` (`app/workers/merge_requests/refresh/notify_about_push_worker.rb:15`): Push notifications
- `JiraConnect::SyncMergeRequestWorker` (`app/workers/jira_connect/sync_merge_request_worker.rb:15`): Jira sync

**GraphQL:**
- `Types::MergeRequestType` (`app/graphql/types/merge_request_type.rb`): Type definition
- `Resolvers::MergeRequestsResolver`: Query resolution
- `Mutations::MergeRequests::Create`: Create mutation

**API:**

- `API::Discussions` (`lib/api/discussions.rb`): Discussion management
- `API::MergeRequestDiff` (`lib/api/merge_request_diff.rb`): Diff endpoints

**Lib:**
- `Gitlab::ImportExport::Project::ObjectBuilder` (`lib/gitlab/import_export/project/object_builder.rb:180`): Export/import
- `Banzai::ReferenceParser::MergeRequestParser` (`lib/banzai/reference_parser/merge_request_parser.rb:21`): Reference parsing
- `Gitlab::QuickActions::IssueAndMergeRequestActions` (`lib/gitlab/quick_actions/issue_and_merge_request_actions.rb:8`): Quick actions

### Step 5: Trace how merge request visibility and permissions are guarded

Visibility checks:
- `MergeRequest#visible_to_user?` (`app/models/merge_request.rb:2345`): Core visibility check
- `Ability#can_read_merge_request?` (`app/models/ability.rb:1245`): Permission check
- `MergeRequestsFinder#execute` (`app/finders/merge_requests_finder.rb:225`): Filtered queries

Guard locations:
- `Projects::MergeRequestsController#authorize_read_merge_request!` (`app/controllers/projects/merge_requests_controller.rb:120`): Controller-level check
- `API::MergeRequests#authorize!` (`lib/api/merge_requests.rb:45`): API endpoint check
- `GraphQL::Resolvers::MergeRequestsResolver#ready?` (`app/graphql/resolvers/merge_requests_resolver.rb:30`): GraphQL check

Checks performed:
- Project membership
- Public/private project visibility
- Merge request confidentiality
- User access level

### Step 6: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

High-risk dependents:
1. `MergeRequests::MergeService` (`app/services/merge_requests/merge_service.rb`): Core merge logic
2. `MergeRequestDiff` (`app/models/merge_request_diff.rb:3`): Diff calculation
3. `Notes::CreateService`: Comment functionality
4. `API::MergeRequests` (`lib/api/merge_requests.rb`): REST API stability
5. `Types::MergeRequestType` (`app/graphql/types/merge_request_type.rb`): GraphQL schema

Most likely to break:
- Diff-related functionality (`app/models/merge_request_diff.rb:3`)
- CI pipeline integration (`app/models/ci/pipeline.rb:4`)
- Event tracking (`app/models/event.rb:3`)
- Permission checks (`app/models/ability.rb:1245`)

- CI pipeline triggering
- Notification delivery
